intake.finding: Clear, narrow, long-standing CSS paged-media gap: authors who set custom @page margins or add page-margin boxes (headers/footers) cannot detect the printer's non-printable edge area, so content is clipped. ChromeStatus entry created 2025-05-28 by the implementer (mstensho), with motivation, explainer, spec, bug, and initial public proposal all linked. Browser/OS already has the inset data internally; the gap is purely that it is not web-exposed.
intake.limitations: ChromeStatus status text was still 'Proposed' and is_released=false at accurate_as_of 2026-06-01, predating the realized stable ship; the manifest launch event (150:Enabled by default) and release notes establish actual shipment.

incubation.finding: Genuine multi-stakeholder incubation. CSSWG issue #11395 opened 2024-12-19; labelled css-page-3 and placed on the CSSWG April 2025 and TPAC 2025 (Thursday afternoon) agendas. The design was openly contested and evolved: original env(unprintable-area-width) proposal, renamed env(safe-printable-inset), with Mozilla offering a counter-proposal (a keyword/descriptor that clamps used margins rather than exposing a raw value). The shipped design (page-margin-safety descriptor in css-page-3) reflects this convergence. Use cases, non-uniform-inset caveat, and alternatives are documented in the explainer.
incubation.limitations: WG resolution detail and the full comment thread were not retrievable from the issue fragment (GitHub issue body only); resolution inferred from closed status + agenda placement + converged spec.

prototype.finding: Implementation existed behind the Blink runtime flag CSSSafePrintableInset from Chrome 139 (explainer states it is testable via the flag or --enable-experimental-web-platform-features), and a WPT testing mechanism was proposed (RFC #233, a <meta name="safe-printable-inset"> for print tests). The explainer carries runnable @page examples. However, the WPT test infrastructure remained unfinished, and there is no evidence of task-based friction testing or framework/server integration beyond the author's own examples.
prototype.limitations: Intent to Prototype thread (S9) and Chromium bug (S12) could not be retrieved; prototype-phase decisions and early security/privacy treatment are inferred from the explainer and Intent to Ship.

developerTrials.finding: The feature sat behind a flag from Chrome 139 to 150, but there is no formal 'In developer trial (Behind a flag)' launch event in the manifest, no attributable developer cohort, and 'Web developers: No signals' in the Intent to Ship and ChromeStatus. No evidence that a varied cohort of developers tried it on real printers and reported back.
developerTrials.limitations: Flag availability is not the same as a developer trial with feedback; no trial findings, partner evidence, or issue themes found.

wideReview.finding: Substantive review occurred and was largely favorable: TAG review #1115 closed with 'Resolution: validated' / 'we largely support the direction' (assigned xiaochengh); Mozilla standards-position #1258 labelled 'position: positive'; spec is in the CSSWG (css-page-3) and is edited by fantasai (Apple). But gaps remain: WebKit standards-position #519 is still open with no position ('No signal'), the CSSWG-drafts issue was filed under css-env-1 by Mozilla/WebKit while the shipped artifact moved to css-page-3, and ChromeStatus records security_review_status and privacy_review_status as 'Pending' as of 2026-06-01.
wideReview.limitations: 'No signal' from WebKit must not be read as neutral or positive. Security/privacy review completion is not publicly confirmed; the explainer's self-review is the only on-record treatment.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial or field experiment. origintrial=false in ChromeStatus; the Intent to Ship states 'Goals for experimentation: None'. The feature went flag (Chrome 139) directly to default-on (Chrome 150). Not-relevant because no field evidence was sought, not because it was untested in any form.
experiment.limitations: A trial could in principle have surfaced real-printer edge cases (e.g., the explainer's own concern about a single very large unprintable edge), but one was not run.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship filed 2026-04-29 by mstensho; received 3 LGTMs (Philip Jägenstedt, Yoav Weiss, Daniel Bratell) with rollout to desktop/Android/WebView at 150, 'Will ship enabled for all users', no //chrome code, iOS excluded. Several prepare-to-ship packets are incomplete: 'Is this feature fully tested by web-platform-tests? = No' with the WPT testing RFC 'stuck in review' (only tentative tests in Chromium's content_shell runner, not portable WPT); 'Interoperability and Compatibility', 'WebView application risks', and 'Debuggability' left as 'No information provided'; security/privacy 'Pending' in ChromeStatus. LGTM1 explicitly accepted shipping without the WPT infra and deferring portable tests.
prepareToShip.limitations: Intent threads are the main evidence; Chromium tracking bug and WPT RFC could not be retrieved to corroborate review/test completion state at branch time.

release.finding: Shipped in Chrome 150 stable on 2026-06-30 as 'Expose unprintable areas with CSS', described accurately (page-margin-safety descriptor to steer clear of unprintable areas) with tracking bug, ChromeStatus and Spec links in the Chrome 150 release notes. Microsoft Edge platform release notes 150 also list page-margin-safety. No deprecations/removals in 150. Release communication matches the actual rollout.
release.limitations: Release notes coverage is accurate but minimal (one paragraph); see evidenceAndCommunication outcome for the docs/MDN/sample gap.

adoption.finding: Only ~3 weeks post-stable at the evidence cutoff. No adoption evidence found: no MDN reference page, no caniuse entry, not in web-features/Baseline ('Missing feature' web feature id), empty docs/sample arrays in ChromeStatus, empty results for production/GitHub usage searches. Secondary community signals exist (a Japanese-language CSS reference notes Chrome 150 page-margin-safety availability). Absence of evidence is not absence of adoption, but nothing defensible exists yet for a niche printing feature.
adoption.limitations: Too early and too niche to expect measurable adoption; the correct posture is unknown rather than low.

support.finding: No public support issues, regressions, or known-problem reports found in the available sources as of the cutoff. Support state is genuinely unmeasured this soon after stable.
support.limitations: No support channel evidence retrieved; cannot assert low or high support burden.

deprecation.finding: New CSS capability, not a restriction/removal of existing behavior. breaking_change=false, enterprise_impact=1 (none). No replacement/migration or reverse-trial work applies. Not-relevant because the feature adds an option, not a deprecation.

developerValue.finding: Addresses a real, well-scoped, long-standing gap in CSS paged media that experts (CSSWG, the Apple-employed spec editor, Mozilla) treated as legitimate; the motivation is concrete (clipped custom headers/footers/content). However, independent developer demand evidence is absent: 'Web developers: No signals', no attributable developer or partner evidence, and the audience (authors who print-formal paged documents) is niche.
developerValue.limitations: Legitimacy from spec experts is not the same as demonstrated developer demand.

endUserImpact.finding: Potential end-user benefit is indirect and author-dependent: when authors adopt page-margin-safety, printed headers/footers/content will not be clipped into the unprintable margin. But end users cannot invoke it themselves, and no realized end-user impact has been measured.
endUserImpact.limitations: No user-impact data; benefit is hypothetical pending author adoption.

adoption.finding: No measurable adoption as of the cutoff (no MDN/caniuse/Baseline, no production usage or case studies, 'Web developers: No signals'). Shipment is not adoption.
adoption.limitations: Feature is ~3 weeks old; unscored rather than failed reflects recency, not hidden adoption.

interoperability.finding: Standards path is credible and cross-engine in design (WG-tracked css-page-3, TAG validated, Mozilla Positive, spec edited by fantasai at Apple). But at shipment the feature is Chrome-only: no other-engine implementation evidence, WebKit standards-position still open with 'No signal' (do not treat silence as support), spec maturity is only a Working Draft / Editor's Draft, and it is not in Baseline. The env()->page-margin-safety rename also means the Mozilla/WebKit position issues were filed against css-env-1 while the ship target is css-page-3.
interoperability.limitations: No Firefox/Safari implementation signals found; positive position != implementation.

implementationQuality.finding: A working Chromium implementation shipped cleanly (flag since 139, default-on in 150, all Blink platforms except iOS, no //chrome code, clean release). The explicit weakness is test infrastructure: the Intent to Ship states the feature is NOT fully tested by web-platform-tests, the WPT print-test RFC (#233) was still in review at ship, and only 'a few tentative tests' live in Chromium's content_shell runner rather than portable WPT. This was acknowledged and accepted by the LGTMers.
implementationQuality.limitations: WPT RFC #233 and the Chromium bug could not be retrieved directly; test status is characterized via the Intent to Ship. No crash/regression data found either way.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Accurate, on-schedule release communication exists (Chrome 150 release notes, Edge platform notes, ChromeStatus). But documentation/enablement is thin for a shipped stable feature: no MDN reference page, no caniuse/web-features/Baseline entry, and the ChromeStatus doc_links and sample_links arrays are empty. Communication leans on release notes + author explainer + spec rather than reference docs or samples.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: A community (Japanese-language) note exists but is a secondary signal; no English devrel article/demo found.

support.finding: No support issues, known-problem reports, or issue-taxonomy evidence available this soon after stable.
support.limitations: No support-channel evidence retrieved; state is unmeasured, not benign.

overall.finding: A legitimate, well-scoped problem taken through genuine multi-stakeholder incubation and review, converging on a WG-tracked design and shipping cleanly in Chrome 150. At the evidence cutoff it is a single-engine launch (WebKit no signal, no other-engine implementation, not Baseline), shipped without portable WPT coverage (an accepted, acknowledged gap), with thin reference documentation and no measurable adoption or end-user impact. Net: sound process and real problem; incomplete interop/testing/docs evidence; too early to judge adoption or impact.
overall.limitations: Overall is mixed because multiple dimensions remain unscored (adoption, end-user impact, support) and interoperability/implementation-quality are genuinely partial.

supports: Feature metadata, owner, blink component, spec/explainer/intent links, stage/launch events, Gecko Positive / WebKit No signal / WebDev No signals, security & privacy reviews Pending, finch CSSSafePrintableInset, rollout desktop/android/webview=150
sources.limitations: Cache accurate as of 2026-06-01; status text 'Proposed'/is_released=false predates the realized Chrome 150 stable ship. Provided as read-only input.

supports: Initial public proposal (2024-12-19), problem framing, env(unprintable-area-width)/env(safe-printable-inset) origins, css-page-3 label, on CSSWG April 2025 + TPAC 2025 agendas, issue closed
sources.limitations: Reader returned the issue body/fragment without the full comment thread; WG resolution text not captured.

supports: Explainer with runnable @page examples, non-uniform-inset rationale (single max value), Mozilla counter-proposal (keyword/clamp) discussion, privacy/security/accessibility self-review, testable in Chrome 139 via CSSSafePrintableInset flag
sources.limitations: No dated release; community signal low (1 star, 1 watcher).

supports: Early TAG review requested 2025-06-23; Closed; labels 'Resolution: validated' / 'we largely support the direction', 'Focus: API design (pending)', 'Review type: WG early review', 'Venue: CSS WG'
sources.limitations: TAG issue body filed with placeholder Mozilla/WebKit URLs (NNN); actual position issues were filed the next day. Comment detail not captured.

supports: Mozilla position request opened 2025-06-24; Closed; label 'position: positive', topic: CSS; reviewer dholbert; references css-env-1 and env(safe-printable-inset)
sources.limitations: Filed against css-env-1 / env() design rather than the shipped css-page-3 page-margin-safety descriptor; project status showed 'Position is proposed' alongside the positive label.

supports: WebKit position request opened 2025-06-24; still Open; labels topic: css / venue: W3C CSS WG; no position label, no assignee (= 'No signal')
sources.limitations: Open issue with no position must not be read as support or opposition; references css-env-1 design.

sources.publisher: W3C CSS Working Group (CSS Paged Media Module Level 3, Editor's Draft)
supports: Final shipped design is the page-margin-safety @page property in section 7.6 'Staying within the printable area'; <safe-printable-inset> used in fixed-dimension margin-box computation (5.3.3); Editor Elika Etemad/fantasai (Apple); Working Draft maturity
sources.limitations: Editor's Draft / Working Draft (unstable); full 7.6 prose is in a single oversized line and was not fully extracted.

supports: Intent to Ship filed 2026-04-29 by mstensho; 3 LGTMs (Jägenstedt, Weiss, Bratell); ship at desktop/android/webview=150; TAG 'Issues addressed'; Gecko Positive / WebKit No signal / WebDev No signals; NOT fully tested by WPT (RFC #233 in review, tentative tests in content_shell only); no //chrome code; Finch CSSSafePrintableInset; 'Goals for experimentation: None'; Interop/compat, WebView risks, debuggability left blank
sources.limitations: Google Groups render; no separate security/privacy review result recorded in the intent body.

supports: Referenced as the Intent to Prototype in the cached record and the Intent to Ship
sources.limitations: UNRETRIEVED: read attempts timed out. Existence/ID confirmed via ChromeStatus and Intent to Ship; content (prototype decisions, early review treatment) not captured.

supports: Chrome 150 stable release date 2026-06-30; 'Expose unprintable areas with CSS' ships page-margin-safety descriptor; tracking bug 368070327; ChromeStatus + Spec links; no deprecations/removals in 150
sources.limitations: One-paragraph item among many 150 features; no MDN/sample/demo links provided.

sources.publisher: Microsoft Edge Web Platform release notes
supports: Edge platform release notes 150 list page-margin-safety as a way to detect where it is safe to print
sources.limitations: Observed via search snippet only; full page not read.

supports: Proposes <meta name="safe-printable-inset"> for WPT print tests (per Intent to Ship characterization)
sources.limitations: UNRETRIEVED: four read attempts failed (timeouts / 500 / fetch errors). Status ('in review') and 'generally positive' feedback characterized solely via the Intent to Ship, not the primary source.

sources.publisher: とほほのWWW入門 (tohoho-web, community reference)
supports: Independent (Japanese-language) community note that page-margin-safety became usable from Chrome 150 (June 2026)
sources.limitations: Secondary community signal; translation/summary, not an authoritative doc; not evidence of adoption.

successes: Identified and scoped a real, durable CSS paged-media gap (authors cannot avoid the non-printable margin area when setting custom @page margins or margin boxes) and drove it through the CSSWG (issue on April 2025 + TPAC 2025 agendas, closed).

Design was iterated through genuine multi-stakeholder review: Mozilla's env()-vs-keyword concern was incorporated, converging from env(safe-printable-inset) on css-env-1 to the page-margin-safety descriptor in css-page-3 edited by fantasai (Apple) — a cross-engine editor signal.

Clean, accurate, on-schedule release: Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) and Edge platform notes both describe page-margin-safety correctly, with 3 LGTMs and an all-platform (desktop/Android/WebView) rollout requiring no //chrome code.
Honest explainer with explicit privacy/security/accessibility self-review, non-goals, and alternatives (including the Mozilla counter-proposal and the print-preview-fit alternative).
failures: Shipped default-on without portable WPT coverage: the WPT print-test RFC (#233) was still 'in review' at ship and tests exist only in Chromium's content_shell runner, not in shared web-platform-tests. The Intent to Ship explicitly answers 'fully tested by WPT? = No' and LGTM1 acknowledged deferring portable tests — a real cross-engine interop/correctness risk.

Single-engine launch with no interop: no Firefox/Safari implementation evidence; WebKit standards-position #519 still open with 'No signal' (despite the spec editor being Apple-employed); not in Baseline; 'Web Feature ID: Missing feature'.

Documentation/enablement gap for a shipped stable feature: no MDN reference page, no caniuse/web-features/Baseline entry, and empty doc_links/sample_links in ChromeStatus. Discovery leans on release notes + author explainer.

Incomplete prepare-to-ship evidence: security_review_status and privacy_review_status 'Pending' in ChromeStatus (accurate_as_of 2026-06-01); Intent to Ship left Interoperability & Compatibility, WebView application risks, and Debuggability as 'No information provided'.
No developer-demand evidence for a niche printing feature: 'Web developers: No signals', no attributable partner/trial evidence, no cohort feedback from the Chrome 139-150 flag period.
counterfactuals: If the WPT print-test infra (RFC #233) had landed before ship, cross-engine test coverage would exist and reduce silent interop drift risk, giving Firefox/Safari a concrete conformance target — at the cost of slipping past Chrome 150.

If WebKit had been engaged to a closed position pre-ship, the 'No signal' gap and Chrome-only reality could have been reduced; the mismatch between an Apple-employed spec editor and a silent WebKit standards-position issue is itself a friction signal an earlier WebKit bug could have surfaced.
If an MDN page plus a runnable sample had been ready at ship, discoverability and adoption would be materially stronger; this absence is a process/DevRel gap, not a product-design flaw.

If a behind-the-flag developer trial with an attributable cohort had been run across Chrome 139-150, it could have produced the missing 'web developer' signal and stress-tested real-printer edge cases (e.g., the explainer's own concern about a printer with one very large unprintable edge) before default-on.

Filing Mozilla/WebKit/TAG reviews against css-env-1 while the artifact shipped as css-page-3 page-margin-safety left the public review trail pointing at a superseded design — re-requesting positions against the shipped descriptor might change the 'No signal' picture.
skillImprovements: prepare-to-ship packet: add a rule that an 'Enabled by default' must not ship with 'fully tested by WPT = No' without (a) an explicit accepted-risk note and (b) a filed, owner-tracked plan to land portable WPT; flag this as a readiness gap in retrospectives.

wide-review check: when the spec editor is employed by engine X but engine X's standards-position issue is still open / 'No signal', surface that mismatch as a concrete review-friction signal rather than treating silence as neutral.

release-readiness check: require either a live MDN reference page or a tracked docs-gap ticket before counting 'release communication / evidenceAndCommunication' as supported; empty doc_links/sample_links arrays should downgrade the dimension.

developer-trials check: features that ship with 'Web developers: No signals' after a flag period should be flagged as having skipped attributable developer evidence (interest -> evaluation -> trial -> production), and prompted to recruit a varied cohort.

review-tracking check: when the shipped artifact moves specs/names (e.g., css-env-1 env() -> css-page-3 page-margin-safety), require re-confirmation that TAG/Mozilla/WebKit positions were re-requested against the final artifact.

regression eval: add this niche-but-shipped CSS printing feature as an ambiguous/weak case to verify the model does not infer adoption, interop success, or end-user impact from a clean on-schedule shipment.

missingEvidence: Intent to Prototype thread (Google Groups; read timed out) — would confirm prototype-phase decisions and early security/privacy treatment.
Chromium tracking bug #368070327 (issues.chromium.org; reads failed) — would show implementation timeline, regressions, and test/review completion.
WPT RFC #233 (web-platform-tests/rfcs; four read attempts failed) — print-test infra status currently characterized only via the Intent to Ship.
Security and privacy review completion (ChromeStatus shows 'Pending' as of 2026-06-01; no public confirmation of completion at ship).
Post-shipment adoption/usage data (Chrome use counters, web-features/Baseline entry, production deployments) — too early and currently 'Missing feature'.
MDN/caniuse reference page — not present at cutoff.
Attributable developer-trial / partner feedback — none found ('Web developers: No signals').
Full prose of css-page-3 section 7.6 (page-margin-safety) — embedded in an oversized single line; only TOC + computation rules confirmed.
